content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejecimus_p113_l15">
								, <gloss>
									expelled</gloss> (with violence); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="emisimus_p113_l15">
								, <gloss>
									let</gloss> [him] <gloss>go</gloss>. The words <foreign xml:lang="lat">vel</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">vel</foreign>
								(<gloss>or, if you like</gloss>) imply that the same act may be called
								by either name. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsum_p113_l15"> , <gloss>of
								his own accord</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verbis_prosecuti_p113_l16">
								may apply as well to kind words of dismissal as to invective. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abiit_p113_l16">,
								simply, <gloss>is
									gone</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="excessit_p113_l16">
								, <gloss>has
									retreated</gloss> before the storm; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="evasit_p113_l16"> , <gloss>has
								escaped</gloss> by stealth; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erupit_p113_l16"> , <gloss>has
								broken forth</gloss> with violence, — a climax of expression, but nearly
								identical in sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="moenibus_p114_l1">
								(dat. following <foreign xml:lang="lat">comparabitur</foreign>), <gloss>
								against</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_p114_l1">
								(adding with emphasis), <gloss>and so</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hunc_quidem_p114_l1">
								, <gloss>him
									at any rate</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sine_controversia_p114_l2">
								, <gloss>without dispute unquestionably</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versabitur_p114_l3">
								, <gloss>will
									be busy</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="campo_p114_l4"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="foro_p114_l4"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="curia_p114_l4"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parietes_p114_l4">:
								observe the narrowing climax. <pb n="380"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="loco_est_p114_l5">
								, <gloss>he
									lost his vantage-ground</gloss>: a military expression, hence the
								simple abl.; § <ref n="AG 428">428</ref>, f (258, a, N.2); cf. B. 229,
								I; G. 390, 2, N.2; H. 463 (414, ii); H-B. 408, 2, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullo_impediente_p114_l6">,
								i.e. his defenders till now could screen him by forms of law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="justum_p114_l7">
								(if retained in the text), <gloss>regular, in due form</gloss>; cf. note
								on latrocinio, p. 109,1. I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_etc_p114_l9">:
								§ <ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, b (333, b); B. 331, V, a; G. 542; H. 588,
								i (540, iv, N.); H.-B. 594, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cruentum_p114_l10">
								(pred.), <gloss>reeking with blood</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivis_nobis_p114_l10">
								(abl. abs.), <gloss>leaving us alive</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civis_p114_l12">:
								acc. plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jacet_p114_l13">,
								etc., <gloss>lies
									prostrate</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="retorquet_oculos_p114_l15">
								begins the figure of a wild beast, which is continued in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faucibus_p114_l15">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profecto_p114_l15">
								, <gloss>no
									doubt</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_quidem_p114_l16">
								, <gloss>which
									really</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_projecerit_p114_l17">:
								see note on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_extulit_p114_l17">,
								1.9, above; for mood, see § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>,3 (341, d); B.
								323; G. 541; H. 588, ii (516, ii); H.-B. 535, 2, a. <milestone n="2" unit="chapter"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="For_followingsection_p114_l19">, cf. Cat. 1, sects. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 2.27">27</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Catil. 2.28">28</ref> where the supposed complaint against
								Cicero for not having put Catiline to death and his reply to it are
								given at greater length. </gloss>
lemma:
For the contents of this and the following
								section

content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qualis_omnis_p114_l19">:
								acc. plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p114_l19">:
								§ <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (3", c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 254, R.2; H. 583
								(511, I, N.1); H.-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_accuset_p114_l20">
								, <gloss>as to accuse</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref> (320); B.
								283, I ; G. 631,2; H. 591, I (503, i); H-B. 521, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ista_p114_l22">:
								for gender, see § <ref n="AG 296">296</ref>, a (195, d); cf. B. 250,3;
								G. 211, R.5; H. 396, 2 (445, 4); H.-B. 326, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interfectum_esse_p114_l23">:
								§ <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, b, N. (288, d); B. 270, 2, a; G. 280,
								R.2; H.-B. 582, 3, a, footnote 2; observe the emphatic position. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p114_l24">:
								for tense, see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl100.13">p. 100, l. 13</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_imperi_p114_l25">:
								see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl104.16">p. 104, l. 16</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_publica_p114_l25">
								, <gloss>the
									public interest</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_multos_p114_l27">,
								etc.: the passages in brackets are probably spurious; it will be
								observed that they merely repeat —the preceding statement in each case. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_p115_l1">
								(causal) <foreign xml:lang="lat">viderem</foreign>, <gloss>seeing</gloss>: its obj. is </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_possem_p115_l1">
								(which is the apod. of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_multassem_p115_l1">)
								; § <ref n="AG 569">569</ref>, a (288, f); B. 270,3; G. 248; H. 619, 2
								(537,3); H-B. 472, c </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_probata_p115_l1">:
								nearly equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum ne vos quidem</foreign>.
								. . <foreign xml:lang="lat">probaretis</foreign>: implying that if <emph>
								they</emph> do not sustain the act, much less will the people at large. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multassem_p115_l2">:
								for fut. perf. of direct; § <ref n="AG 589">589</ref>, 3 (337, 3); B.
								319, B; G. 657, 5; H. 646 (527, i). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_ut_p115_l2">
								, <gloss>the
									result would be that</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_possetis_p115_l4">,
								result clause explaining </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huc_p115_l4"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videretis_p115_l5">:
								§ <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324, I G. 663, I; H. 652 (529, ii);
								H.-B. 539; if not dependent on <foreign xml:lang="lat">
									possetis</foreign>, it would be videbetis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_quidem_p115_l5">
								, <gloss>whom,
									by the way</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="intellegatis_p115_l6">:
								§ <ref n="AG 565">565</ref> (331, i); B. 295, 6; G. 553, 2; H. 564, ii, I
								(499, 3); H-B. 531, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_exierit_p115_l7">:
								§ <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3(341, d); B. 323; G. 539; H. 588, ii
								(5I6, ii); H-B. 535, 2, N.2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mihi_p115_l9">:
								eth. dat.; as if, <gloss>I notice</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 380">380</ref>
								(236); B. 188, 2, b; G. 351; 11.432 (389); H.-B. 372. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aes_alienum_p115_l10">,
								etc., i.e. petty debts run up in cook-shops and the like; not like the
								heavy mortgages spoken of afterwards. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reliquit_p115_l11">:
								notice the emphatic position. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quos_viros_p115_l12">:
								for a characterization of Catiline's partisans, see sects. 18-23. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prae_p115_l14"> , <gloss>in
								comparison with</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gallicanis_p115_l14">,
								i.e. those permanently stationed in Cisalpine Gaul. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">ager Gallicus</foreign> below was that strip of
								sea-coast north of Picenum formerly occupied by the Senones, but at this
								time reckoned a part of Umbria. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_dilecta_p115_l15">
								refers to a levy recently raised. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Q_Metellus_p115_l15">
								(Celer): see note on Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.19">19</ref>
								, <ref n="pl107.13">p. 107, l. 13</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senibus_p115_l17">,
								etc., i.e. those classes who naturally look forward to a revolution to
								mend their fortunes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="luxuria_p115_l18">
								= <gloss>
									high-livers</gloss>: abstract for concrete, as common in Latin and
								older English; Cf. Shakespeare, <title>All's Well</title> ii. I, 91:
								"Bring in the admiration"(i.e. this wonderful person). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vadimonia_deserere_p115_l19">
								, <gloss>desert</gloss> their bondsmen, i.e. leave them in the lurch in
								their creditors' suits. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="edictum_praetoris_p115_l21">,
								in effect like a <gloss>sheriff's writ</gloss>. Any official order of a
								magistrate was an <foreign xml:lang="lat">edictum</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hos_p115_l22">, as
								opposed to those he did take out. <pb n="382"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fulgent_purpura_p115_l24">,
								i.e displaying their rank as Senators, who alone had the right to wear
								the broad purple stripe (<foreign xml:lang="lat">latus clavus</foreign>)
								on the tunic. The reference, therefore, is to foppish young nobles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mallem_p115_l24">:
								§ <ref n="AG 447">447</ref>, I (311, b); B. 280, 4; G. 258, and N.1; H.
								556 (486, i); H.-B. 519, I, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eduxisset_p115_l24">:
								§ <ref n="AG 565">565</ref>(331, f a.); B. 295, 8; G. 546, R.2; H. 565, 2
								(499, 2); H.-B. 5;9, I, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_permanent_p115_l24">:
								a future condition; § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, a, N. (307, a, N.);
								G. 228; H. 533, 2 (467, 5); H.-B. 571. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mementote_p115_l25">,
								i.e. let them remember that they are objects of suspicion and shall be
								watched accordingly. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_hoc_p115_l27">,
								etc., i.e. their effrontery makes them still more a cause for alarm. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="video_p115_l28">,
								i.e. I know perfectly well. - </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cui_sit_p115_l29">,
								etc.: cf. Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.9">9</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superioris_noctis_p115_l31">,
								i.e. three nights ago. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_p116_l3"> , <gloss>
								surely</gloss>: an affirmative particle sometimes wrongly spelled <foreign xml:lang="lat">nae</foreign>. <milestone n="116.5" unit="pg_l"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_videretis_p116_l3">:
								clause of result explaining </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p116_l3"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_vero_p116_l7">:
								ironical (as usual), introducing a <foreign xml:lang="lat">reductio
									ad
									absurdunt</foreign>. (The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_p116_l7"> only
								doubles that in <foreign xml:lang="lat">nisi</foreign>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_jam_p116_l8">
								, <gloss>no
									longer</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Aurelia__via__p116_l11">:
								see Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.24">24</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rem_publicam_p116_l13">:
								§ <ref n="AG 397">397</ref>, d (240, d); B. 183; G. 343, 1 H. 421 (381);
								H.-B. 399 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sentinam_p116_l13">
								, <gloss>
									refuse</gloss> (see Cat. 1, <ref n="pl104.22">p. 104, l. 22</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejecerit_p116_l14">:
								the conclusion is implied in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="O_fortunatam_p116_l14">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exhausto_p116_l14">
								, <gloss>drained
									off</gloss> (cf. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sentina_p116_l14">
								). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recreata_p116_l15">
								, <gloss>
									invigorated</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="toti_Itali_p116_l17">:
								§ <ref n="AG 429">429</ref>, 2 (258,1 2); B. 228, I, b; G. 388; H. 485, I
								(425, 2); H.-B. 436, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subjector_p116_l18">
								, <gloss>
									forger</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="circumscriptor_p116_l18">
								, <gloss>
									swindler</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perditus_p116_l21">
								, <gloss>abandoned
									wretch</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hosce_p116_l22">: § <ref n="AG 146">146</ref>, a, N.1 (101, footnote); B. 87, footnote 2; G.
								104, i, N 1; H. 178, 2 (186, I); H.-B. 138, 2, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="asciverit_p117_l3">:
								for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c (287, c); B. 268, 6; G.
								513; H. 550 (495, vi); H.-B. 481. <milestone n="5" unit="chapter"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possitis_p117_l4">:
								§ <ref n="AG 532">532</ref> (317, c); B. 282, 4; G. 545, R.3; Cf. H.
								568, 4 (499, 2, N.) ; H.-B. 502, 2, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diversa_studia_p117_l4">.
								In another passage (<ref n="Cic. Cael. 13">Cael. 13</ref>) Cicero
								ascribes to Catiline: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Cum tristibus severe,
									cum
									remissis jucunde, cum senibus graviter, cum juventute comiter,
									cum
									facinorosis audaciter, cum libidinosis luxuriose vivere</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ratione_p117_l4">
								, <gloss>in different directions</gloss>. <pb n="383"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ludo_p117_l5">,
								the regular <gloss>training-school</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gladiatorio_p117_l5">:
								see Cat. 1, <ref n="pl110.31">p. 110, l. 31</ref>, and note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="levior_p117_l7">,
								etc.: the Roman actors, though some of them achieved distinction, were
								generally regarded as a low class of men. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p117_l8">,
								i.e. though a companion of such dissolute persons, yet he possessed the
								qualities of fortitude and endurance so much admired by the Romans. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exercitatione_p117_l9">

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adepti_sint_p121_l27">,
								for the fut. perf. indic. of the direct disc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fugitivo_p121_l27">,
								i.e. one of their own slaves; for, when law is overthrown, brute force
								will control all. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sit_necesse_p121_l28">:
								§ <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, d (307, d); G. 595; H. 580 (508, 4); cf.
								H.-B. 582, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_coloniis_p121_l31">:
								Sulla rewarded his veterans (120,000 in number) by liberal grants of
								land, partly in <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipia</foreign> already
								existing, partly in new colonies which he founded for them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="universas_p122_l1">
								, <gloss>as
									a whole</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civium_esse_p122_l1">
								, <gloss>consist
									of</gloss> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_coloni_p122_l2">
								, <gloss>these
									are colonists of this sort</gloss> (as opposed to the general
								character of the colonies, which Cicero does not wish to impugn). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="beati_p122_l5"> , <gloss>men
								of wealth</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Sulla_p122_l7">,
								etc., <gloss>Sulla
									will have to be raised from the dead</gloss>, for they can have no
								such hope in Catiline. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agrestis_p122_l8">
								, <gloss>
									farmers</gloss>, not Sulla's colonists. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veterum_p122_l9">:
								alluding to the plunder of the disorderly times following Sulla's
								victory over the Marian party. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illorum_temporum_p122_l12">,
								i.e. the times of proscription. <milestone n="10" unit="chapter"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vacillant_p122_l18">
								, <gloss>stagger
									under</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vadimoniis_p122_l18">,
								etc.: the three steps in bankruptcy,—<gloss>bail, judgment, and sale
									of
									property</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">proscriptio</foreign> is
								strictly the public notice that property is for sale. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infitiatores_lentos_p122_l21">
								, <gloss>dilatory debtors</gloss> (lit. <gloss>deniers</gloss>, i.e.
								persons who avoid payment of their debts by every possible subterfuge). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="stare_p122_l22"> , <gloss>keep
								their feet</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_p122_l23"> , <gloss>in
								such a way</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_modo_p122_l23">,
								etc.: § <ref n="AG 217">217</ref>, e (149, c); B. 343, 2, a; G. 482,5,
								R.1; H. 656, 3 (552, 2); H.-B. 299. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_revoco_p122_l29">:
								§ <ref n="AG 467">467</ref> (276, b); B. 259, 2; G. 233; H. 530 (467, 6);
								H.-B. 484. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="carcer_p123_l1">:
								this is the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Tullianum</foreign>, a dungeon near
								the Forum, still existing. It was properly a jail for temporary
								detention, as imprisonment was not recognized in Rome as a form of
								punishment. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="numero_p123_l2"> , <gloss>in
								order</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="genere_p123_l2"> , <gloss>in
								rank</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imberbis_p123_l5">:
								a mark of effeminacy; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bene_barbatos_p123_l5">
								, <gloss>
									full-bearded</gloss>, doubtless a military affectation, as, until
								lately, the wearing of a mustache. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="velis_p123_l6"> , <gloss>
								veils</gloss>, rather than the substantial <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign>, which was of unbleached wool. The
								whole description suggests foppishness and effeminacy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="saltare_cantare_p123_l11">,
								these accomplishments were hardly regarded as respectable by the better
								classes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="spargere_p123_l11">,
								i.e. in food or drink: poisoning has in all ages been carried to a high
								art in Italy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scitote_p123_l13">:
								notice the second (fut.) imperat. (regularly used in this word). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_noctibus_p123_l16">:
								although this was spoken Nov. 9, yet the Roman year was at this time in
								such a state of confusion that the true date was probably some time in
								December, just when the winter was setting in.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbes_coloniarum_p123_l27">,
								etc.: the colonies and free communities (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municipia_p123_l27">)
								included the walled cities (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbes_p123_l27">)
								in their territory. These well-manned walls would be more than a match
								for Catiline's rude works (<foreign xml:lang="lat">tumulis</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causas_p124_l1">,
								i.e. the cause of the conspirators and that of the state in their moral
								aspect (cf. in <foreign xml:lang="lat">eius modi</foreign>, etc., 1.12,
								below). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_ipso_p124_l2">
								, <gloss>from
									the very comparison</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bona_ratio_p124_l10">
								, <gloss>good
									counsel</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perdita_p124_l10">
								, <gloss>
									desperate</gloss>. <milestone n="12" unit="chapter"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="custodiis_vigilisque_p124_l17">:
								see Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.8">8</ref> and note. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consultum_p124_l19">,
								etc., <gloss>
									provident</gloss> measures have been taken. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="coloni_municipesque_p124_l19">:
								a colony differed from a <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipium</foreign> in
								being founded by Roman (or Latin) citizens, who retained from the first
								their citizenship, either in whole or in part. By Cicero's time there
								was no longer any real difference between the two classes of towns; but
								the colonies always retained a certain precedence in rank. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hac_excursione_p124_l20">:
								see Introd., p. 113 of text. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gladiatores_p124_l22">:
								see <ref n="pl117.5">p. 117, l. 5</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_p124_l23">
								(corrective), referring to <foreign xml:lang="lat">manum
									certissimam</foreign>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p124_l24">:
								pointing the contrast between the suppression of this body and
								Catiline's expectations from them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vocari_videtis_p124_l29">:
								the members of the Senate had their gathering place (<foreign xml:lang="lat">senaculum</foreign>) adjoining the <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign>, and were summoned by heralds (<foreign xml:lang="lat">praecones</foreign>) from this into the building. If
								any were absent, the heralds were sent to their houses. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">senaculum</foreign> were visible from the place of
								assembly in the Forum, and the heralds could no doubt be seen going
								their rounds. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="monitos_volo_p125_l2">:
								497, c, N. (292, d, N.); G. 537; H.-B. 605, 3. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="solutior_p125_l3">:
								for compar., see § <ref n="AG 291">291</ref>, a (93, a); B. 240, I G.
								297; H. 498 (444, I); H.-B. 241, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p125_l4">,
								etc., <gloss>as
									for the rest</gloss>, i.e. what remains to be done. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horum_p125_l5">
								and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_p125_l5">
								relate to the citizens by whom he is surrounded, and imply a gesture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cujus_p125_l8">:
								referring, like <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui</foreign>, to the subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">sentiet</foreign>. <milestone n="13" unit="chapter"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_p125_l17">,
								etc., abl. abs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="togato_p125_l17"> , <gloss>in
								perfect peace</gloss>, i.e. without any military demonstration: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign> was the regular dress of the Roman in
								time of peace. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_p125_l22">:
								in appos. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possitis_p125_l22">:
								I <gloss>will secure that</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_que_p125_l24">
								, <gloss>not... and</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_ducibus_p125_l28">
								, <gloss>under whose guidance</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_hanc_p126_l3">
								, <gloss>this city which</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 307">307</ref>, b, N.
								(200, b, N.); G. 616; H. 399, 5(445, 9); or (repeating the noun) <gloss>the
								city which</gloss>, etc.,— <gloss>
									<emph>that</emph> city</gloss>. </gloss>
